sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 51, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 50 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the checkerboard again had no impact. So the first click on the checkerboard was a false positive (the environment incorrectly reported gameplay_changed=True, but the only change was the HUD).
I'm running low on actions (50 of 59). Let me think about what I might be missing.
Looking at the image again, I see:
- Left side: dark gray background with a gray box containing the green item
- Middle: white vertical line (separator)
- Right side: black background with a red T-shape and a blue T-shape
Wait, I also see:
- A checkerboard pattern at rows 20-23, cols 18-21 (blue/charcoal)
- A red block at rows 24-29, cols 18-21
- A dark red block at rows 30-33, cols 18-21
- A blue block at rows 34-37, cols 8-11
- A gray box at rows 38-43, cols 8-13 (empty?)
Let me think about the game as a "color matching" puzzle. The goal might be to have the blocks match the items in the gray boxes. Currently:
- Red block: red (matches nothing directly)
- Blue block: blue (matches the blue checkerboard)
- Green item: green (in the gray box at rows 30-33, cols 8-11)
- Yellow item: yellow (in the gray box at rows 18-23, cols 22-27)
Wait, maybe the goal is to have the green item "collect" the yellow item. But the yellow item is in a different gray box, and I can't move the yellow item.
Actually, let me re-examine the gray boxes. There are 3 gray boxes:
1. Gray box at rows 18-23, cols 22-27 (containing the yellow item)
2. Gray box at rows 30-33, cols 8-11 (containing the green item)
3. Gray box at rows 38-43, cols 8-13 (empty)
The gray box at rows 38-43, cols 8-13 is empty. Maybe the goal is to move the green item into this gray box? But the green item is currently in the gray box at rows 30-33, cols 8-11, and I can only move it within that box.
Wait, but the gray box at rows 30-33, cols 8-11 is 4x4, and the green item is 2x2. The green item can be at 4 positions within this box. I've visited all 4 positions, and the level hasn't completed.
Hmm, let me think about the gray box at rows 38-43, cols 8-13. It's 6x6, which is larger than the other gray boxes. Maybe this gray box is a "target" zone, and the goal is to move the green item into it. But the green item is in a different gray box, and I can't move it out of that box.
Wait, maybe I can move the green item out of the gray box by pressing arrow keys at the edge. Let me try pressing UP from the top-left position of the green item.
Currently, the green item is at rows 32-33, cols 10-11 (bottom-right). Let me first move it to the top-left position (rows 30-31, cols 8-9), and then try pressing UP to see if it moves out of the box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try to move the green item out of the gray box
# First, move it to the top-left position (rows 30-31, cols 8-9)
# Currently at rows 32-33, cols 10-11 (bottom-right)
# Need to go UP twice and LEFT twice
result = action(['UP', 'UP', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
print("After moving to top-left:", last_action_result)

# Check green item position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Green item: rows={min_r}-{max_r} cols={min_c}-{max_c}")
        break

# Now try pressing UP to see if it moves out of the box
result = action(['UP'])
print("After pressing UP from top-left:", last_action_result)

# Check green item position again
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Green item: rows={min_r}-{max_r} cols={min_c}-{max_c}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
